title: Torsion Induced Asymmetric Luttinger Liquids
abstract: We consider a general model of a Luttinger liquid with broken parity and time reversal symmetry, but with their composite symmetry intact. Such a scenario can be due to a combination of torsion and a Zeeman field in nanowires, or a result of bringing different helical Luttinger liquids into proximity. The broken symmetries result in a band structure with no axis of symmetry, and therefore with asymmetric velocities between left and right moving contributions. By taking a general spin-full model with all possible scattering and interaction terms in the bosonic model we show that generically the spin degree of freedom becomes gapped out, resulting in an effective spinless Luttinger liquid with asymmetric velocities. Our work generalizes and extends previous studies which focused on a minimal model of a spinless Luttinger liquid. We further demonstrate that a possible experimental signature of the asymmetry of such asymmetric models can be seen in the spectral function.
summary: This paper explores a novel class of 'asymmetric' Luttinger liquids where left- and right-moving particles travel at different speeds due to torsion and magnetic fields. By generalizing previous spinless models to a spinful framework, the authors demonstrate that the spin sector becomes gapped, leaving an effective spinless liquid. They provide a concrete way to detect this effect experimentally by looking for an asymmetric signature in the spectral function. This work is significant for understanding transport in complex 1D geometries like twisted nanowires.
main_problem: The study investigates how the breaking of parity (P) and time-reversal (T) symmetries, while preserving composite PT symmetry, affects the properties of a spinful Luttinger liquid, specifically focusing on the emergence of asymmetric left- and right-moving velocities.
main_result: The authors show that in a general spinful model, the spin degree of freedom generically becomes gapped out, resulting in an effective spinless Luttinger liquid with asymmetric velocities, and identify a non-zero odd component in the spectral function as a definitive experimental signature of this tilt.
method: The researchers use bosonization, renormalization group (RG) analysis, and the calculation of Green's functions to derive the effective low-energy theory and spectral properties.
model_or_system: The model describes a 1D spinful electronic system, such as a helical nanowire, subject to torsion and a Zeeman field, which induces a velocity asymmetry between chiral modes.
key_observables: The spectral function A(k, omega), specifically the even and odd combinations (Ae and Ao) of the spectral function.
important_parameters: Torsion (tau), Zeeman field (B), velocity asymmetry (delta v), and Luttinger parameters (Kc, Ks).
main_assumptions: The model assumes a smooth/adiabatic limit where curvature and torsion vary slowly relative to the Fermi wavelength, and relies on a first-order RG analysis.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the projected band dispersion and the scaling of velocity asymmetry with torsion and Zeeman field; Figure 2 provides a schematic of the model; Figures 3 and 4 illustrate the asymmetry in the spectral function and the non-zero odd component signature.
paper_structure: The paper begins with a microscopic derivation of the asymmetric Hamiltonian from a helical wire, proceeds to a bosonized description and RG analysis to show the gapping of the spin sector, and concludes with the calculation of the spectral function to identify experimental signatures.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Microscopic extraction of the tilt from the exact projected lower band. In panel (a) the exact lower-band dis- persion ϵ−(k) for τB > 0, τB < 0, and τ = 0, are shown. For the representative asymmetric case, the tangents at the two Fermi points k−and k+ are shown explicitly, demonstrating unequal chiral slopes v−̸= v+. Reversing the sign of τB re- verses the asymmetry. In panel (b) the velocity asymmetry or tilt, δv = (v−−v+)/2, extracted numerically from the exact Fermi-point slopes is plotted versus τB. The odd dependence and near-linear behavior close to the origin confirm the mi- croscopic scaling δv ∝τB.
FIG. 2. Schematics of the g′ 2 and g′ 1 backscattering processes, as labeled on the figures. Pictured are those non-oscillating processes which lead to the massive term Eq. (35).

FIG. 3. The spectral functions for q > 0, top panel, and q < 0, bottom panel. Relatively large values of the asymmetric velocity vac are used for illustrative purposes. For plotting the spectral function has been convoluted with a Gaussian resolution function of width ω/vc|q| = 0.02.

FIG. 4. The odd spectral function, see Eq. (71). A non-zero odd spectral function is a demonstration of the tilt. For plot- ting the spectral function has been convoluted with a Gaus- sian resolution function of width ω/vc|q| = 0.02. See teh caption of Fig. 3 for more details.
